title: Howl's Moving Castle - Chapter Eight: In which Sophie leaves the castle in several directions at once (Fragment 2)
text:
Howl realized something was wrong with her. He jumped indoors across his guitar, took hold of her elbow, and sat her in the chair. “Take it easy now!” Something happened between Howl and Calcifer then. Sophie felt it, because she was being held by Howl, and Calcifer was still leaning out of the grate. Whatever it was, her heart began to behave properly almost at once. Howl looked at Calcifer, shrugged, and turned away to give Michael a whole lot of instructions about making Sophie keep quiet for the rest of the day. Then he picked up the guitar and left at last.

Sophie lay in the chair and pretended to feel twice as ill as she did. She had to let Howl get out of sight. It was a nuisance he was going to Upper Folding as well, but she would walk so much more slowly that she would arrive around the time he started back. The important thing was not to meet him on the way. She watched Michael slyly while he spread out the spell and scratched his head over it. She waited until he dragged big leather books off the shelves and began making notes in a frantic, depressed sort of way. When he seemed properly absorbed, Sophie muttered several times, “Stuffy in here!”

Michael took no notice. “Terribly stuffy,” Sophie said, getting up and shambling to the door. “Fresh air.” She opened the door and climbed out. Calcifer obligingly stopped the castle dead while she did. Sophie landed in the heather and took a look round to get her bearings. The road over the hills to Upper Folding was a sandy line through the heather just downhill from the castle. Naturally. Calcifer would not make things inconvenient for Howl. Sophie set off toward it. She felt a little sad. She was going to miss Michael and Calcifer.

She was almost at the road when there was shouting behind her. Michael came bounding down the hillside after her, and the tall black castle came bobbling along behind him, shedding anxious puffs of smoke from all four turrets.

“What are you doing ?” Michael said when he caught up. From the way he looked at her, Sophie could see he thought the scarecrow had sent her wrong in the head.

“I’m perfectly all right,” Sophie said indignantly. “I’m simply going to see my other sister’s granddaughter. She’s called Lettie Hatter too. Now do you understand?”

“Where does she live?” Michael demanded, as if he thought Sophie might not know.

“Upper Folding,” said Sophie.

“But that’s over ten miles away!” Michael said. “I promised Howl I’d make you rest. I can’t let you go. I told him I wouldn’t let you out of my sight.”

Sophie did not look very kindly on this. Howl thought she was useful now because he wanted her to see the King. Of course he did not want her to leave the castle. “Huh!” she said.

“Besides,” said Michael, slowly grasping the situation, “Howl must have gone to Upper Folding too.”

“I’m quite sure he has,” said Sophie.

“Then you’re anxious about this girl, if she’s your great-niece,” Michael said, arriving at the point at last. “I see! But I can’t let you go.”

“I’m going,” said Sophie.

“But if Howl sees you there, he’ll be furious,” Michael went on, working things out. “Because I promised him, he’ll be mad with both of us. You ought to rest.” Then, when Sophie was almost ready to hit him, he exclaimed, “Wait! There’s a pair of seven-league boots in the broom cupboard!”

He took Sophie by her skinny old wrist and towed her uphill to the waiting castle. She was forced to give little hops in order not to catch her feet in the heather. “But,” she panted, “seven leagues is twenty-one miles! I’d be halfway to Porthaven in two strides!”

meta.author: Diana Wynne Jones
meta.sectionTitle: Chapter Eight: In which Sophie leaves the castle in several directions at once

vocabulary.definition: Deeply interested or involved; preoccupied.
vocabulary.example: She was absorbed in her book.

vocabulary.definition: A person's way of standing or moving; also a reference point or direction.
vocabulary.example: He checked his bearings before continuing the journey.

vocabulary.definition: Leaps or jumps from one place to another, often indicating speed or distance.
vocabulary.example: The dog came in bounds to greet me.

vocabulary.definition: Nervous and confused.
vocabulary.example: He got flustered during the interview.

vocabulary.definition: Desperate or wild with excitement, passion, fear, pain, etc.
vocabulary.example: She made frantic attempts to call for help.

vocabulary.definition: Behaving in a hurried, anxious, and somewhat downhearted manner.
vocabulary.example: She worked in a frantic, depressed sort of way after the bad news.

vocabulary.definition: Extremely angry.
vocabulary.example: He was furious when he found out.

vocabulary.definition: Not to be allowed to leave one’s presence or supervision.
vocabulary.example: The manager told the apprentice not to get out of his sight.

vocabulary.definition: Comprehending or understanding the facts or circumstances.
vocabulary.example: She is grasping the situation quickly after hearing the news.

vocabulary.definition: A metal frame in a fireplace to hold fuel while allowing air to circulate.
vocabulary.example: The fire glowed brightly within the grate.

vocabulary.definition: Easily annoyed or restless due to delay or opposition.
vocabulary.example: He grew impatient waiting for the bus.

vocabulary.definition: In a manner indicating anger or annoyance at something perceived as unfair.
vocabulary.example: Sophie protested indignantly when Michael doubted her.

vocabulary.definition: To remain silent or not reveal information.
vocabulary.example: You must keep quiet about the surprise.

vocabulary.definition: To move so as not to be seen.
vocabulary.example: They looked out of sight before the meeting started.

vocabulary.definition: Wrote down important information.
vocabulary.example: She made notes during the lecture.

vocabulary.definition: A person, thing, or circumstance causing inconvenience or annoyance.
vocabulary.example: The noise is a real nuisance.

vocabulary.definition: In a willing or helpful manner.
vocabulary.example: The waiter obligingly brought her extra napkins.

vocabulary.definition: Completely confident or certain about something.
vocabulary.example: I’m quite sure the answer is correct.

vocabulary.definition: An object made to resemble a human figure, set up to scare birds away from crops.
vocabulary.example: The field was guarded by a scarecrow.

ru.translation: ботинки семимильные (сказочные ботинки, которые позволяют делать очень большие шаги)

vocabulary.definition: Magical boots enabling the wearer to travel great distances in few strides.
vocabulary.example: He put on the seven-league boots and crossed the land quickly.

vocabulary.definition: Walking in an awkward, slow, or shuffling manner.
vocabulary.example: She shambling towards the exit with tired steps.

vocabulary.definition: To emit small clouds of smoke.
vocabulary.example: The old engine shed puffs of smoke as it started.

vocabulary.definition: To raise one’s shoulders briefly to express doubt, ignorance, or indifference.
vocabulary.example: She shrugged when asked about the problem.

vocabulary.definition: To seize or grab something firmly.
vocabulary.example: She took hold of the rope tightly.

vocabulary.definition: A small tower on top of a larger structure, often on a castle.
vocabulary.example: The castle turrets were visible from miles away.
